behaviors.description: The content passed to the summary-generation step includes data retrieved from all three sources.
behaviors.justification: The failure boundary is at the aggregation orchestration (ensuring all three sources contribute to the output). A Unit test of a single function misses whether all three sources are wired into the aggregator. Mocking the three data sources and verifying the aggregator output contains their data catches the real failure (missing source). System-level real HTTP is unnecessary—we're testing aggregation logic, not source reliability.
behaviors.test_description: Mock all three data sources (metrics endpoint, issue tracker, deployment log) to return known datasets. Call the aggregation function and verify the output dict contains keys/values from all three sources in the structure expected by the summarizer.

behaviors.description: When one data source is unavailable, the report is produced with a `data unavailable` note for that section rather than aborting.
behaviors.justification: The failure boundary is the graceful-degradation logic: exception → catch → add note → continue. Integration-level testing with a mocked source raising an exception verifies this path. Risk: real source unavailability (network timeout vs. auth error) might raise an exception type not covered by error handling, causing MOCK-HIDE. System-level testing (real unreachable service) is more robust, but Integration suffices to verify graceful-degradation logic itself.
behaviors.test_description: Mock one data source to raise a connection exception. Call the aggregation function and verify: (1) no unhandled exception is raised, (2) the output includes a `data unavailable` entry for that section, (3) data from the other two sources is present and correct.

behaviors.description: The narrative summary accurately represents the key values present in the input data.
behaviors.justification: If the summary is LLM-generated ('narrative' suggests this), correctness depends on what the real LLM produces—outputs are non-deterministic and the model might paraphrase, omit, or misrepresent values. Mocking the LLM to return a fixed, 'correct' summary doesn't verify the real LLM is accurate; it only tests plumbing. To catch the real failure (LLM produces inaccurate output), test with the real LLM (Agentic). If summarization is rule-based/deterministic, Unit would suffice, but LLM-based is more likely given context.
behaviors.test_description: Call the real summary generator with input data containing known key values (e.g., '42 critical issues', '3 deployments'). Verify the generated narrative includes accurate, recognizable representations of these values. If LLM-based, run multiple times to check for consistent accuracy across non-deterministic outputs.

behaviors.description: A complete report — from data collection through email delivery — reaches the configured recipient's inbox correctly.
behaviors.justification: The real failure is 'report doesn't reach recipient's inbox.' Integration-level mocking of the email service only verifies 'we called send()'—it misses real delivery failures (SMTP errors, auth failures, mailbox quota, network issues). Email delivery requires real SMTP, authentication, and the recipient's mailbox. These are System-level concerns (real HTTP/API, real external service). Only System-level testing (real SMTP or test email service like mailhog) verifies the report actually reaches the inbox.
behaviors.test_description: Set up a test SMTP server or test email service (e.g., mailhog running locally). Configure the system to send to a test mailbox. Execute the full report pipeline (collect data, generate summary, send email). Verify the email appears in the test mailbox with correct subject, recipients, and report content.

behaviors.description: When the email delivery service is unreachable, the generated report is saved to a local file and delivery is retried on the next scheduled run.
behaviors.justification: The failure boundary is the fallback/retry logic: service fails → exception → catch → save to file → schedule retry. Integration-level testing with a mocked unreachable email service (raises exception) and verification of file-save and retry setup logic catches this. Risk: real service failures (SMTP timeout, auth error) might raise exception types not caught by the handler (MOCK-HIDE), making System-level testing more robust. But Integration suffices to verify the fallback logic itself.
behaviors.test_description: Mock the email service to raise a connection exception. Call the full report pipeline. Verify: (1) no unhandled exception propagates, (2) the report is written to the configured local file with correct content, (3) retry logic/metadata is initialized. Optionally: mock the email service to succeed on second call, manually trigger the scheduled task again, and verify the saved report is read and resent.
